revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:Sekku]][[bn:গোসেক্কু]][[de:Sekku]][[en:Gosekku]][[hr:Go-sekku]][[it:Gosekku]][[ja:節句]][[ko:셋쿠]][[ru:Сэкку]][[uk:Ґосекку]]
{{Short description|Five annual ceremonies of the Japanese imperial court}} {{More citations needed|date=February 2016}} [[File:Gosekku-zu by Kakizaki Hakyo (Hakodate City Museum).jpg|thumb|right|265px|''Gosekku-zu'' by [[:en:Kakizaki Hakyō|Kakizaki Hakyō]], with {{ill|hagoita|en|hagoita|qid=Q508435|lt=hagoita|1=Hagoita}} for the first month, peach blossoms for the third, irises for the fifth, the two lovers for the seventh, and chrysanthemum motifs for the ninth; from the collection of {{ill|Hakodate City Museum|en|Hakodate City Museum|qid=Q11480647|lt=Hakodate City Museum|1=Hakodate City Museum}}]] {{Italic title|reason=}} The '''''Gosekku''''' (五節句), also known as ''sekku'' (節句), are the five annual ceremonies that were traditionally held at the Japanese imperial court. The origins were Japanese practices merged with Chinese practices and celebrated in Japan since the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} in the 8th century CE. The Japanese culture and tradition incorporated this in a unique way that spread throughout the country. The festivals were held until the beginning of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. Some of them are still celebrated by the public today.<ref>{{Cite web|title=History of Yusoku Hina Ningyo Dolls|url=http://www.kyoto-shimazu.com/en/History_of_Yusoku_Hina_Ningyo_Dolls.html|access-date=2021-08-21|website=www.kyoto-shimazu.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Enjoying the Go-sekku (The Five Seasonal Festivals): May 5, Tango no Sekku|url=http://shun-gate.com/en/power/power_style_64.html|access-date=2021-02-21|website=SHUN GATE|language=en}}</ref>
*''Kochōhai'': on New Year's, the nobles processed before the emperor during the ''{{ill|Jinjitsu|en|Jinjitsu|qid=Q13423431|lt=Jinjitsu|1=Jinjitsu}}'' celebrations. *''Kyokusui'': on the third day of the third lunar month, courtiers floated rice wine down a stream in the palace garden. Each guest would take a sip and then write a poem. The ''{{ill|Hinamatsuri|en|Hinamatsuri|qid=Q844110|lt=Hinamatsuri|1=Hinamatsuri}}'' festival continues today. *''Ayame no hi'': on the fifth day of the fifth month, {{ill|Mugwort|en|Mugwort|qid=Q15114159|lt=Mugwort|1=Mugwort}} was hung to dispel evil spirits. Celebrated as the {{ill|Japanese iris|en|Japanese iris|qid=Q16753983|lt=Japanese iris|1=Japanese iris}} (ayame) festival at court, today it is known as ''{{ill|Tango no sekku|en|Tango no sekku|qid=Q18578715|lt=Tango no sekku|1=Tango no sekku}}''. *''Kikkoden'': on the seventh day of the seventh month, offerings were made during the ''{{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|qid=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}}'' festival, which celebrated the annual crossing of the Weaver ({{ill|Vega|en|Vega|qid=Q3427|lt=Vega|1=Vega}}) and Cowherd ({{ill|Altair|en|Altair|qid=Q12975|lt=Altair|1=Altair}}) constellations. *''Chōyō no en'': on the ninth day of the ninth month, a celebration was held that originally featured chrysanthemum wine, but later became associated with the autumn rice harvest. It is today known as the ''[[:en:Kiku no sekku|Kiku no sekku]]''.
The artist {{ill|Ikeda Koson|en|Ikeda Koson|qid=Q11551796|lt=Ikeda Koson|1=Ikeda Koson}} (1801-1866) painted five hanging scrolls in around 1830, which depict the festivals.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asia.si.edu/collections/edan/object.php?q=fsg_F1999.5.4&bcrumb=true |title=Object | Online | Collections | Freer and Sackler Galleries |website=Asia.si.edu |date=2013-03-15 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306223319/http://www.asia.si.edu/collections/edan/object.php?q=fsg_F1999.5.4&bcrumb=true |accessdate=2016-02-25|archive-date=2016-03-06 }}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}} * Arai Hakuseki, Joyce Ackroyd (trans.), ''Told Round a Brushwood Fire'', University of Tokyo Press (1979), 286n122.
{{Authority control}}
